# Dependency Locking
*or what to Do When Today’s Build Doesn’t Match Yesterday’s*

This scenario demonstrates Gradle's Dependency Locking feature.

#### Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.+]
```

#### Gradle (Groovy DSL)

```{.groovy include="/demos/gradle-demo/scenario-11-locking/build.gradle" snippet="s11-deps"}
```

#### Expected Intuition
"It should stay the same."

#### Actual Resolution Results
Uses `gradle.lockfile`.

#### Classpath Reality
Locked version.

#### Why This Happens
Gradle's dependency locking records the exact resolved versions of all dependencies into a gradle.lockfile (per-project) or a single lock file for all configurations. On subsequent builds, Gradle uses these recorded versions instead of performing dynamic resolution. This converts dynamic versions into fixed versions without changing your build scripts.

#### How This Scales in Real Systems
Dependency locking is essential for any team that values reproducible builds, which is to say, every professional team. It is particularly critical in CI/CD pipelines, where builds must be deterministic to enable reliable testing, deployment, and rollback. Lock files should be committed to version control. Updating dependencies becomes an explicit action (./gradlew dependencies --write-locks) rather than an accidental side effect of a repository change. Many organisations combine locking with automated dependency update tools (like Dependabot or Renovate) that submit pull requests when new versions are available.

#### Signals to Watch For
If you see "locking not activated" warnings but use dynamic versions, you're at risk. If a build produces different results on different machines or at different times, locking is the first thing to check. Use ./gradlew dependencies --write-locks to regenerate lock files after intentional dependency changes.

#### Key Takeaway
Locking ensures reproducibility.

#### Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 9: Ranges](../scenario-9-ranges/guide.md)
